did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an Improved Bag of Features (IBOF) algorithm combined with Speeded Up Robust Features (SURF) for feature extraction, K-means clustering for visual vocabulary creation, and a LIBSVM classifier to detect and classify skin diseases (acne and boil) from digital images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing an 'Improved Bag of Features Algorithm' for skin disease detection. It details a specific research methodology including dataset creation (data augmentation of acne and boil images), feature extraction (SURF), clustering (K-means), and classification (LIBSVM). It reports original results such as 96% accuracy, 98.48% confidence rate, and precision/recall metrics, and describes the development of a mobile application prototype. Although published in a conference proceedings, it contains original technical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Disease Detection Using Improved Bag of Features Algorithm
journal: 5th Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems
brief_summary: This study developed a model to detect and classify common skin diseases, specifically acne and boil, using digital image processing. The researchers employed an Improved Bag of Features (IBOF) algorithm, utilizing Speeded Up Robust Features (SURF) for feature extraction and K-means clustering to create a visual vocabulary. The dataset consisted of 200 augmented images for each class, with 90% used for training and 10% for testing. The system was evaluated using a LIBSVM classifier, achieving an overall accuracy of 96% and a total average confidence rate of 98.48%. Additionally, the combined average precision and recall rate for both categories was 99%, with acne showing the highest precision at 98%. The study also resulted in the development of an Android mobile application prototype for practical skin disease detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two common skin diseases studied and classified in the experiment. The dataset contained 200 augmented images for this class. It achieved the highest precision (98%) and highest number of correct predicted skin diseases in the confusion matrix.

One of the two common skin diseases studied and classified in the experiment. The dataset contained 200 augmented images for this class. It achieved a precision of 97% and the lowest number of correctly classified images among the two classes.

all_task_types.details: The paper develops a model to detect and classify common skin diseases, specifically acne and boil, using digital image processing. The system uses a LIBSVM classifier to predict discrete classes. The study reports classification metrics including overall accuracy (96%), precision (98% for Acne, 97% for Boil), recall (99% for both), and confidence rates. The output is a class label (Acne or Boil) rather than a continuous numeric value.

datasets_used.name: Custom Acne and Boil Dataset
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset created by the authors consisting of digital images of acne and boil skin diseases. The images were collected and then subjected to data augmentation to increase the sample size. The specific source of the original images (e.g., hospital, public repository) is not reported.

use.training: Used for training the Improved Bag of Features (IBOF) model and creating the visual vocabulary via K-means clustering. 90% of the augmented images for each class were used.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's accuracy, precision, recall, and confidence rates. 10% of the augmented images for each class were used. Additionally, 4 test images from each category were used for confidence testing.
use.other: The dataset was used to develop and validate an Android mobile application prototype for skin disease detection.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the reliability of detection and classification results on test data.
To quantify the error of the model's predictions.
To evaluate classification performance by counting correct and incorrect predictions for each class (Acne and Boil).
To measure the accuracy of positive predictions and the ability to find all relevant instances for each class.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported descriptive performance metrics such as accuracy (96%), loss (0.03), confidence rate (98.48%), precision, and recall.

Custom Acne and Boil Dataset.notes: The model achieved the highest number of correct predictions for Acne. Boil had the lowest number of correctly classified images among the two classes. The total average confidence rate of 98.48% was derived from tests done with different test data. Specifically, for 4 test images per category, Acne had an average confidence rate of 99.97% and Boil had 99.68%, resulting in a total average of 99.83% for those specific tests, while the overall total average confidence rate for detection and classification was reported as 98.48%.

architecture_used.main_model: Improved Bag of Features (IBOF) algorithm with LIBSVM classifier

feature_extractor_details.interest_point_detector: Hessian matrix approximation using box filters (9x9, 15x15, 27x27)
feature_extractor_details.integral_images: Used for fast computation of box type convolution filters
feature_extractor_details.descriptor: 64-dimensional descriptor vector based on sums of Haar-wavelet responses
feature_extractor_details.descriptor_window: 20x20 square region centered around the interest point, divided into 4x4 subregions
feature_extractor_details.orientation: Dominant orientation chosen by rotating a circle segment covering an angle around the origin

architecture_details.clustering: K-means clustering used to group descriptors and create a visual vocabulary (visual dictionary)

classifier_details.parameters: Default parameters used for accuracy and efficiency

training_details.data_augmentation: Applied to the dataset (200 images per class: Acne and Boil)

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation applied to the dataset (increasing sample size to 200 images per class for Acne and Boil)
Creation of Integral Images for fast computation of box type convolution filters
Gaussian filtering (smoothing with Gaussian kernels and sub-sampling in scale-space representation)
Non-maximal suppression for interest point localization
Interpolation to sub-pixel accuracy for accurate interest point localization

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the custom dataset to increase the sample size to 200 images for each class (Acne and Boil). The specific geometric or color transform methods used for this augmentation are not reported in the text.

conclusions: The Improved Bag of Features (IBOF) algorithm combined with Speeded Up Robust Features (SURF) and LIBSVM classifier effectively detects and classifies common skin diseases (acne and boil) with high performance.
The developed model achieved an overall accuracy of 96% and a total average confidence rate of 98.48% in detecting and classifying skin diseases.
The combined average precision and recall rate for both acne and boil categories was 99%.

Acne classification achieved the highest precision (98%) and the highest number of correct predictions, while boil classification had a precision of 97% and the lowest number of correctly classified images among the two classes.
Both acne and boil models achieved a recall rate of 99%.
The study successfully resulted in the development of an Android mobile application prototype for practical skin disease detection.

The system demonstrates potential as a reliable and effective computer-aided medical diagnosis tool for skin conditions, addressing issues such as financial barriers and hesitation to seek hospital care.
important_remarks: The study is limited to only two skin conditions (acne and boil), restricting its generalizability to other dermatological pathologies.
The dataset is small (400 total images) and custom-built with unspecified sources, raising concerns about population bias and lack of external validation.
Data augmentation was applied to increase sample size to 200 images per class, but the specific augmentation techniques (e.g., rotation, flipping) are not reported.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the performance metrics.
The source of the original images is not disclosed, making it impossible to assess potential selection bias or data leakage risks.
The system was evaluated using a small subset of test images (4 per category) for confidence rate calculations, which may not robustly represent real-world performance.
The study was published in a conference proceedings rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may indicate less rigorous scrutiny compared to journal publications.

The model relies on traditional computer vision techniques (SURF, IBOF, LIBSVM) rather than deep learning, which may limit its ability to capture complex features compared to modern CNN-based approaches.
